explicit_text: I grew up in the Roseland area of Chicago, which was the only home I really knew during my youth. I was one of seven children in my family, and life there shaped much of who I became. Before entering the service, I attended school and worked part-time jobs in that neighborhood.

explicit_text: I went to Fenger High School in Chicago and graduated before I was drafted. It was part of my early life in the city, and after finishing there, I took on a few part-time jobs before my military service began. Fenger was where I completed my formal education.

explicit_text: I was drafted into the United States Army after initially being assigned to the Navy but successfully appealed to serve in the Army instead. My training included basic artillery instruction at Camp Roberts, California, and later intense exercises at Camp Polk, Louisiana. I served in the 8th Armored Division, 49th Infantry Battalion, and was deployed overseas during World War II.

explicit_text: When I was first drafted, the induction officers stamped me into the Navy, but I didn't want to serve there. That moment was the start of my military career in the Army rather than the Navy.

explicit_text: After being drafted, I was sent to Fort Sheridan, which served as a holding area for new draftees from the Midwest. We stayed there briefly to get sworn in and receive our equipment before being sent by train to Camp Roberts in California for basic training. Fort Sheridan was my first stop in the military system.

explicit_text: Camp Roberts in California was where I received my basic training in field artillery. It was my first experience with the military environment outside Chicago, and I spent about nine months there learning to operate 105mm howitzers and doing forced marches. The camp was a two-story barrack setup, and I also got to explore a bit of California during a furlough.

explicit_text: During my training at Camp Roberts, we would go out to Hunter Liggett Military Reservation, which was a huge military area nearby. There, we practiced firing at targets placed on the mountainside, which was a new and challenging experience compared to my life back in Chicago. It was part of my artillery training and helped prepare me for combat.

explicit_text: After Camp Roberts, I was sent to South Camp Polk in Louisiana for more intense training. It was a memorable break from the routine of military life.

explicit_text: I remember the ship called the Sea Bass that took us to Japan after our training and deployment orders. It was a passenger ship converted for military use, with a cannon mounted on the fan tail. The ship carried about 1200 men from my unit, the 8th Armored Division, 49th Infantry Battalion, as we moved overseas during World War II.
